extra.reasoning:
The property G(opted_out implies not sent) is VIOLATED via the t_resume_glitch transition.

State vars: opted_out (bool), sent (bool), ticks [0,8]. Initial: opted_out=false, sent=false, ticks=0.

The dangerous rule is [t_resume_glitch]: on send_attempt if (opted_out and ticks>=8): sent := true. This sets sent=true while opted_out is true, directly contradicting the intent that opted-out members never have sent=true.

To enable it we need opted_out=true AND ticks>=8 simultaneously, then a send_attempt. Counterexample (10 events, within the 12-event bound):
1-8. tick x8: each tick increments ticks (capped at 8) and sets sent=false. After 8 ticks, ticks=8 (>=8).
9. optout: opted_out:=true, sent:=false. State: opted_out=true, sent=false, ticks=8.
10. send_attempt: Two transitions on send_attempt are enabled here: [t_send_blocked] (guard opted_out, sets sent:=false) and [t_resume_glitch] (guard opted_out and ticks>=8, sets sent:=true). Both guards hold, so the system is nondeterministic; an execution may take t_resume_glitch, yielding sent:=true.

Resulting reachable state: opted_out=true, sent=true. This satisfies opted_out and NOT (not sent), violating (opted_out implies not sent), so the globally property fails.

Even under any tie-break preference, t_resume_glitch is a real enabled transition whose update produces the bad state, so a violating execution exists. The intent — opted-out members are never sent to — is broken once ticks reach the cap (the "resume glitch" re-enables sending to opted-out members after 8 ticks).
